
@yzhliu @lanking520 That is correct. We are autogenerating the code in the `gen` directories using the `/dev/generator.clj` code. This code gets invoking using a command line `lein generate-code` and produces these files by inspecting the classes created by Scala with java reflection. Where the scala package creates these classes using macros directly into memory without persisting, the clojure package chose to persist them to files to help the user discover what functions are available, generate api documentation, and to be able to see what new functions were added via a git diff from release to release.

I doesn't matter much, but just for our SemVer reflexion, I wonder what kind of version bump was the most appropriate here.

On the one hand, it's mostly internal business: the behaviour of the variables will stay the same, they are just declared in another way. That would be an argument towards a patch release.

On the other hand, if a contributor has written a reform using a `build_column`, his.her code would be broken now. That could even push for a major release...

I guess the main problem is the fact that `base.py` explicitly list the functions to import from core. It may be something to clean up to improve dependency management.

No need for a class variable here. This will just look like this:

```python
@lazyproperty
def header(self):
    """
    Return the |Header| object representing the default header for this
    section. A |Header| object is always returned, whether such a header is
    present or not. The header itself is added, updated, or removed using the
    returned object.
    """
    return Header(self._sectPr)
```

A few remarks:

* I order methods alphabetically within the class; that saves a full scan when looking for a particular one later.
* Every method gets its full docstring on creation. Same with classes. One never seems to get back to it later :)
* You'll need a Header class definition for this to pass. Which means we'll need a new file docx/header.py.

I notice there's a .clear() method on TabStop in the MS API. I'll accept a .clear() method on our TabStop object into the API if you like. That's consistent with the TabStops.clear() method, although they call that .clear_all() in the MS API. I suppose, on balance, it makes sense to rename TabStops.clear() to TabStops.clear_all(). That reduces risk of confusion with variable names that only differ by a single character (tab_stops.clear() vs. tab_stop.clear()). Then both these methods would be named the same as in the MS API, which is a consistency we've generally maintained throughout the library.

.remove() has an established Python meaning in the context of a list, so I wouldn't be wanting to choose a different name for that on the TabStops object, which is essentially a custom list.

Python does not have asynchronous server calls implemented.  In the case you describe the thread sends request to server, waits for response, interprets response of server error, adds task back to queue, then reaches the while loop. In this case the len of the queue is 1 and the _error is not set. 

Currently _error is only set if we reach a 1 size chunking error in which case a resubmission will not account.  With the assumption that 'all tasks are of similar size' this is the only early termination scenario I know of as we likely want it to attempt all adds in the case of other client errors. _error should probably be redefined to _has_early_termination error to make this more clear of a distinction from any error.

I don't see how this can happen because the async stack ends on the waiter for `Future` and there are no done callbacks associated with it, see this example: https://ideone.com/izIfqw

Think of how this would work, currently no-one sets an exception on this future besides cancelling the waiter at [1], we can verify this by seeing the future is only available from `self._waiters` and no one sets an exception on it.

so, if you cancel task at [1], you have two options:

1) you don't wait on the cancelled task:
  1.1) you can release it (ok, removed from list), and then wait on cancelled (item already gone from list)
  1.2) you can not release it (ok), then wait on cancelled (item will be removed from list)
2) you wait on the cancelled task, in which case the baseexcept runs and removes item from list.  Nothing else can happen because we're just waiting on the future and no-one else is waiting on said future or has callbacks against said future.

I don't think this is even close:
- actually to make the server down because of the "slow" response (we speak of a 575ms response, which is not good but not even close to be sufficient to make the server down easily), you have to reach the congestion point of the reverse-proxy + the application. Even at this point, traffic will only slow down but it will be served and without any human intervention, it will disappear simply because ressources are freed after each call.. Human intervention will be necessary to make the problem go away faster.
- the parameter problem is already present: the admin will generate a cache entry for each call, it doesn't need to be an intentionnal exploit. And it's not rate related: you don't need X requests/second to make it happen, you only need to make enough requests in `server_timeout` (1 hour by default) to fill the memory. And once this point is reached, redis (in our case) will simply lock and all is down (already happen more than once this is also why I'm really carefull about it). One of the main differences, there is no restart possible without human intervention to empty the cache. Ressources are not freed after each call but after TTL. And by the way, the fact that cache isn't used because the cache key is different also mean that we still have the initial poor performance issue on each call.

Throttling won't solve the issue, it will only limit it. Even with one request/second I think we can fill the cache quite easily in 1 hour because each request add the entire payload in memory for TTL without reuse.
